sentences.text: Well, Atlit is a detention camp close to Haifa, or not far from Haifa, which usually has been used, as far as I know, for the immigrants.
And especially the illegal immigrants frequently had to stay there for several months before they were finally allowed into the mainstream.
It was also used as a quarantine, I think, for legal immigrants who had to stay there for three or four weeks sometimes.
And it-- it was really not-- not unpleasant.
I don't have-- except for the last couple of days, I don't have any bad memories of it.
I don't remember anything even bad about the food.
And I mean, we had beds.
And we were, I don't know how many people were.
I don't remember how many.
I have stayed in so many huts that I don't remember how many people in a hut were in Atlit.
But I would say maybe 16 or so.
It-- it wasn't too terribly crowded.
And we were there for about 10 days.
So I, as I said, I should have looked up the date.
And I don't remember the exact date.
But we were told-- it was sometime the end of November, the beginning of December.
We were told that we had to pack, and that tomorrow morning we were going to be deported to the island of Mauritius.
Nobody knew anything about Mauritius.
The only thing I knew was that there was a very rare stamp, the Blue Mauritius.
And I remember I think either my brother or my father sometimes-- sometime have-- hadn't-- had mentioned it.
But that was about it.
So the-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] the people who were in charge of the transport decided that we are not going to leave without a fight.
And so they decided that we offer passive resistance.
And we were all instructed not to dress, stay naked, not to pack.
And when the police comes in the morning, they might be embarrassed to see us, especially the women.
And maybe we can accomplish staying in Palestine that way.
I think it was a rather primitive way, but it was felt that we shouldn't leave without a fight.
So, obviously, we followed instructions.
And we went to bed undressed.
And we stayed under the covers until the morning.
And during the night, the camp was surrounded by police and military.
We were really heavily surrounded.
And I remember waking up-- I mean, I don't think we slept a wink during the night.
But I-- I remember the morning came.
There was nothing, and there was nothing, and there was nothing.
It was already 6:00 and 7:00 and 8:00 and still nothing.
And we didn't know what had happened.
Nobody moved until, I don't remember the exact time, but it was after 8:00, until we finally heard screaming and yelling.
And apparently, the military and the police had entered the huts of the single men, which-- who usually stayed separately somewhere.
And they just dragged them out of bed, naked or not naked.
They beat them up pretty heavily, blood was flowing all over, and dragged them out.
And there was big screaming and yelling and crying and what have you.
Until an hour later, it was finally decided that the whole effort was futile.
And people started dressing and taking their luggage and packing it.
And we were instructed to put the luggage in front of the door, that somebody was going to pick it up.
And so people started leaving.
And so we finally decided to leave, too.
So we got dressed.
But we did one very stupid thing, a friend of mine and I.
We, out of spite, decided not to pack our belongings and left them in the middle of the hut, whereas everybody was good and packed their belongings and put them out in front of the door.
And so we left the camp.
She and I, we left the camp with just the things on our body and a couple of things in my pocket.
And then we came to the place in-- in Haifa where they examined us and looked whether we didn't have any knives or stuff like that.
But they decided to take some of our stuff.
And they-- they really stole.
They stole.
I had a fountain pen.
They took away my fountain pen.
Don't ask me why.
They took away snapshots.
I had my glasses in my pocket.
I used glasses for distant vision.
They took my glasses.
And I was left with nothing.
But my-- I had those-- oh, what do you call them-- breeches, and boots, and a shirt, and a sweater, and a handkerchief.
That was all.
That's how I got on the boat.
And unfortunately-- but that is later-- unfortunately, Israel was cold at that time, because it was winter.
It was December.
And Mauritius was the height of the summer.
And so I came in this attire to Mauritius without anything else, which was very unpleasant.
I can tell you that.
But let's start with the boat.
There were two also I think luxury liners.
They were Dutch, the New Zealand and the Johan de Witt, which were waiting in the harbor for us.
And we were loaded on the New Zealand.
It was a very beautiful boat.
However, they-- since they punishing us, obviously, for what we had done, so they stuck us again into the bunker or the catacombs.
And it was terrible.
My god.
I remember that first night.
There was barely room.
We were lying on the floor, and there was barely room between the two people.
It was unbearably hot.
I will never forget that.
This-- this was, I think, worse, almost worse, than the storm on the Atlantic.
We couldn't breathe there.
There were like maybe 100 people, you know, stuffed together in one room, which I don't know how big it was.
But it certainly was not big enough for the amount of people who were there.
So that's where we slept.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] continued to be punished.
The next morning, they shaved all the men's hair.
And they cropped the women's hair completely short, telling us that they wanted-- didn't want any lice.
But there was really no particular reason, because we had been in Atlit for a week or 10 days, and we were perfectly clean.
But as I said, they wanted to punish us.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was pretty hot also on the boat, because we weren't allowed to go on deck for the first several days.
And I remember the only thing that we wanted was water.
And the water was very warm.
And it was very difficult to get ice water, but sometimes we managed to get a little ice.
The crew was very nice.
They mainly Malaysian.
And some, I think, Filipinos were there, too.
But they were very nice to us and really tried to help if they could.
I mean, they couldn't do very much.
But they brought us some water sometimes.
And I went-- one night I went out with a friend of mine.
We went and hoped we would not see anybody.
And we got to a bathroom, which was not locked.
And we went into that bathroom and jumped into the tub, the two of us.
And it was the most glorious experience I remember.
But anyhow, they-- the British, there were-- I don't remember how many police were with us.
It was mainly Palestine, I think only Palestine police, who were sort of guarding us.
And they were really very mean, most of them.
They, for instance, for the first week or so, they, in-- in spite of the ship being huge and empty apart from us, they crowded us together in certain areas.
And they gave the men and women an open latrine together.
So for the first three or four days, it was terrible, honest to god.
I think most of the people got constipated because they didn't want to go to the latrine.
And only after three or four days, they finally allowed a curtain between the two sides.
They-- they-- you know, it was-- that was unnecessary, what they did.
But I guess they wanted to show us you were  bad boys and girls, so we got to punish you.
After about four or five days, it got a little better.
And they allowed us to go on deck.
And it was able to survive.
The food was really quite good.
But most of us didn't feel like eating.
Most-- we were really pretty down.
And I know I was sick.
I had a fever.
And I had a terrible cold.
And then I got a terrible rash from-- it must have been some allergic rash, because I remember I was going nuts trying to scratch myself.
Finally, I saw-- had to see a doctor.
And actually, the doctor was quite nice.
He gave me a couple of shots.
And eventually after three or four days, the rash went away.
But that was really bad.
So we were all pretty desperate.
Nobody knew what was going to be in Mauritius.
Nobody knew where Mauritius was, Nobody knew nothing.
And so that was it.
We were-- actually, we were going with a convoy, zig-zagging.
I mean, this was war.
And we went through the Suez Canal and by East Africa.
And after, I think we were 17 days, if I remember correctly, we were on the sea.
And then we arrived in Mauritius.
And actually, it was very interesting.
I remember the first impression of Mauritius was great.
I mean, green, and lots of colors.
It was the middle of summer.
And when we got into the harbor, there were lots of people standing there and greeting us, lots of boats coming to our ship and screaming and yelling and waving.
You know, nobody knew why, because they really didn't know who we were.
And-- but it was sort of nice.
It was a pleasant surprise.
And so we stood in the harbor for, I think, a day or two.
And then they finally got us out.
And the harbor is in Port Louis, which is the capital of Mauritius.
And the camp where they were taking us is about, I think, maybe 10 miles from there.
It's in the city of Beau-Bassin, which is a small-- it's really not a city.
It's like a large village or small town.
And that's where the camp was.
The camp was an old prison with 'His Majesty's Prison,' big letters on top.
And it consisted of two blocks, block houses, which had separate cells.
I think, if I remember correctly, each house had about 400 single cells.
And that's where the men went.
And the women went into a-- the women's camp, which consisted of, again, the corrugated iron sheet barracks, about 32 to one barrack.
And the women's camp and the men's camp was divided by a huge wall.
And there were walls around each of the camps separately and then a division wall in between with a gate.
And we came to Mauritius in December.
It was unbearably hot.
I never forget that.
And on top of it, it was raining.
Mauritius, it rains all the time.
A friend of mine coined the expression, in Mauritius it rains with every [? where. ?] And it's true.
It was really strange.
You could go and-- and here was sunshine.
And then you go over there maybe 20 feet and it rained.
It was a lot of rain in Mauritius.
Mauritius is a-- a tropical island, which is about, I think it's-- it's in the southern hemisphere.
It's about, I think, 20 degrees south.
And the weather actually is really not quite tropical, because it's surrounded by the sea.
But it's terribly humid.
And that's one of the worst things about it, and hot, and mosquitoes.
Yeah.
It-- Mauritius was, I believe, discovered by the Portuguese sometime in the 1600s.
Then the Dutch took over and had it for maybe 100 years.
And it's called actually after the Dutch stadholder, Prince Maurice of Nassau.
That's what Mauritius was called after.
And after the Dutch, the French took over during the Napoleonic Wars, and then the British.
And it-- in our time, it was a British colony.
And now it's independent, has been independent since the mid '60s, I think.
It's about 800 square miles, the whole island.
So it's really very, very small, and any number of little cities and towns there.
The capital, as I said, is Port Louis.
And the-- there's lots of jungle there, too, really real jungle.
And-- but it's really beautiful.
It's now a very, very good holiday place.
Lots of people-- I-- I have met so many people now who have gone to Mauritius on holidays.
You can't believe it.
When we were in-- in Europe a couple of years ago, in Vienna there are placards all over.
You want to have a nice holiday, go to Mauritius, honest to god.
And it's-- it's really a very, very beautiful island.
Except the climate is, as I said, not too bad.
Now apparently, not too good.
Now apparently it's mosquito-free.
In my time, there were lots of mosquitoes.
And we had lots of malaria.
That was one of the worst things.
I had malaria 13 times.
Yeah.
The inhabitants of Mauritius are mainly Indians who somehow had come over to build something or do something.
In my time, there were about 500,000 people living on Mauritius, out of which there were about 200,000 Indians.
The were about 20,000 Chinese, and about 1,500 French still, remnants, really, the-- the richest people in Mauritius.
What happened?
What-- what was life like in the Mauritius camp?
Well, at the beginning, I remember mainly that it was terribly hot and very unpleasant.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were quite a few sick people that we had brought with us, which really was sickness still from the Atlantic, obviously.
And at least 100 people were immediately hospitalized.
I forgot to mention that there were-- I think we were like 1,500 people who got onto Mauritius.
About 100 people were hospitalized immediately with the definite suspicion of typhoid fever.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I belonged, obviously, to the people who didn't have a profession and really didn't know anything except for a little field work.
But I decided I would like to help at the hospital.
They were asking for volunteers.
And so a friend of mine and I decided that we'll give it a try.
We had no idea.
We had never, obviously, worked with sick people, and never worked in a hospital.
But we were definitely needed, and so we started working with the hospitalized people, who most of them had typhoid fever.
And it was pretty rough.
We were-- the hospital, I forgot also to mention, was about-- was within the camp grounds and was about probably maybe 500 yards away from the main camp.
So in the morning, we had to usually go there.
We needed the permission to leave the women's camp.
But obviously, it was granted to go to the hospital.
And we worked there, if I remember correctly, like I think 8 to 10 hour shifts.
And our main duties were feeding the patients, giving them a bedpan, and washing them, and things like that.
We-- it was very, very sad to work at that hospital because so many people died.

We had about, during the first months on  Mauritius, we probably had at least 50 and maybe-- possibly-- possibly 100-- I knew that-- those numbers, but I somehow I forgot them-- dead who died of typhoid fever.
And it was very many young people.
I remember several of them that I was helping who died a couple of days later.
I remember especially one guy who was about 21 years old.
It was terrible.
It was interesting that the Czech transport had gotten vaccination against typhoid.
And nobody of us got it.
So it was all the others who got typhoid fever, who were not vaccinated.
So even at that time, the vaccinations apparently helped.
I remember having gotten three vaccinations still in Prague before-- in Bratislava before we went on the boat.
So the life in the camp in general eventually became like the life in any small village or town.
Most people did something, like I worked in the hospital.
There were people who were carpenters, who worked as carpenters.
And there were people that were doctors.
There were quite a few professional.
There were seamstresses.
Anything you can think of was really there.
Payment, of course, was minimal.
Most people didn't have any money, even though eventually quite a few people got money somehow from relatives or families who were able to send them some.

And the people who worked made a little bit of money, either from other camp inmates who had the money or if they worked for the camp, they were paid, again, a minimal, minimal, salary, maybe $3 a month or something like that through the camp authorities.
And there were people who gave lessons.
There were people who sold coffee, made coffee and sold coffee.
And there was really lots of stuff available in this respect.
Eventually, some people worked on the outside.
There were very skilled mechanics who were for awhile allowed to work outside to-- to make decent money that way.
But there were not too many who were allowed to work outside.
There was a central kitchen.
We were fed through the central kitchen.
The food was terrible.
I remember, my god.
There was polenta, which I hated, lots of carrots, which I hated.
There was really bad bread.
That was one of the few things that I liked.
And we had meat once a week.
And we had some-- something like Pam once a week-- Spam.
Is it called Spam?
Spam, something like that, once a week, some very fatty corned beef which also-- from a can, which also tasted terrible.
And really, lots of people had really trouble with the food.
The only thing which was great, there was really good fruit-- good fruit on the island.
We didn't get too much, bananas here and there.
But again, if we had a little money, we had a little canteen and so we can buy some of the fruit.
There were pineapples, and guavas, and mangoes, and papayas.
And that-- that was good.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] As far as the general life is concerned, I think almost everybody found a little niche somewhere and did something.

The worst part I think, of the stay of Mauritius, at least during the first couple of years, was the so-called family life, which was nonexistent because men and women were not allowed to intermingle except two or three times a week in the afternoon for two hours on some recreation grounds.
And since we had quite a few married couples, it was really difficult for them.
And it is still thought today that it was really unnecessary hardship which was put upon us.
It was-- since it was changed eventually, it could have been changed quite at the beginning and people wouldn't have to have suffered.
We had a commander who was, I think, a little bit sadistic.
And so he tried to do certain things which were not right, like after people complained about the family-- I mean, it didn't bother me.
I was too young and I was really not interested in family life.
But it bothered mainly the married couples.
And since the commander was begged and-- and-- and pleaded with to do something about it, finally he decided to make one change, which I thought was awful.
They-- in the-- in the recreation grounds, they put up tents.
And so three times a week when you went there for the two hours, so you could enter the tent and do your job and-- and sort of be there for half an hour, then change course, somebody else came in.
It was awful.
I'll never forget that.
It was  really awful.
There were some families who had kids, small kids.
So the kids were sitting in front of the tent, watching their parents go in and come out again.
That was really terrible.

But after about two years, or a year and-- I think about a year and a half, it was permitted for the married women to go to the men's camp at-- I think it started out with 11:00 in the morning and come back home at 6:00 at night.
And later on, it was changed to 9:00 in the morning until 9:00 at night.
So after that started--
